title: Test if file is readable from within podman container gives unexpected result
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm not sure if this is podman problem or maybe the way how I spin up the container. I see different result of `[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"` when executed within podman container and when executed in docker container. I used `/etc/fstab` just as example, but I see this same behavior when trying any existing file, no matter if it's owned by user within container or by root. I tested this within unprivileged rootless container with root user within container as well as non-root user within container.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

```bash
podman run -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"'
```

**Describe the results you received:**

Nothing is printed even though tested file exists.

**Describe the results you expected:**

'!' should be printed

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Following results in expected behavior:
```bash
docker run -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"' # prints '!'
```

Also locally:

```bash
[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!" # prints '!'
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.0
API Version:  3.3.0
Go Version:   go1.17
Git Commit:   98f252a3a1a8f1ee00f9f96c6ba00500954b5093-dirty
Built:        Sat Aug 21 13:48:18 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


comments.body: I can't replicate locally, seems to be working fine here. Can you provide more details on your environment?

Hi @mheon , I run on Arch Linux.

Not sure if this may be relevant but I have this parameter in my kernel parameters: `systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0`

I have fuse-overlays installed.

I think I am running on cgroups v1 (I was not disabling it in favor of cgroups v2)

I have set `kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1` in sysctl.d and created subuid and subgid

What else would be useful?

I tried on one of my servers and noticed the same outcome as I observed on my laptop. I run older version of podman there, but I have similar setup in terms of cgroups version. I don't have `systemd.unified_cgroup_hierarchy=0` kernel parameter in my boot options. I do have `kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1` in sysctl.d
@Luap99 thanks for linking, indeed this seems to be duplicate. Sorry for reposting, I was using wrong search keywords and did not see the issue you linked.

I was too quick to close this issue, it seems my `seccomp.json` is already in sync with `seccomp.json` linked within https://github.com/containers/crun/issues/646#issuecomment-819437630 . Maybe this solution does not apply to cgroups v1

I have updated my system but the issue persists.

```
Version:      3.3.1
API Version:  3.3.1
Go Version:   go1.17
Git Commit:   4c5283fabff2de5145838f1847a5a7b2b1fbc0a5-dirty
Built:        Wed Sep  1 19:27:46 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

Any chance you can try with runc instead of crun, on the off chance it's an OCI runtime issue?

My next guess after that would be a potential fuse-overlayfs issue - does root Podman also exhibit this issue?

Hi @mheon - when running as root the behavior is as expected (`!` is printed as expected, from my example above).

I will try with runc, though need to read how to do this first and not screw my system. Remember there were problems with runc when I was looking at this originally on my Linux Arch installation.
Have it installed shouldn't affect things as long as `crun` is also available, we'll default to `crun` as long as it is available.

I switched to `runc` and the problem is not there when using `runc`. I can't remember why I have `crun` left in my configuration as I remember I was testing my local podman installation with `runc`, so I think for now I will switch to `runc`. Thanks!

I am not able to reproduce locally:

```
$ podman --runtime /usr/bin/crun run -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"' # prints '!'
!
$ sudo podman --runtime /usr/bin/crun run -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"' # prints '!'
!
```

@grzegorzk could you show me the output for `stat /etc/fstab` and `cat /proc/self/mountinfo` inside the container where the command fails?

Could you strace the container process and attach here the output you get from strace?:

You can do that with:
```
$ podman run -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash
```

And from another terminal do:

```
$ strace -f -vv -s 5000 -p $(podman inspect -l | jq .[0].State.Pid)
```

Then back to the container and run the command that fails for you.

also, can you try running with `--security-opt seccomp=unconfined`.

If that works, can you verify what libseccomp version you've installed?

Running with seccomp=unconfined yields correct result:

```
[grzegorz@lenovo ~]$ podman run --security-opt seccomp=unconfined -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"'
!
```

libseccomp version:

```
[grzegorz@lenovo ~]$ find /usr/lib | grep libseccomp
/usr/lib/libseccomp.so.2.5.2
```

thanks!

so the failure is coming from:

```
faccessat2(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/fstab", R_OK, AT_EACCESS) = -1 EPERM (Operation not permitted)
```

libseccomp seems ok.  It might be an outdated seccomp profile.

Could you fetch the file https://raw.githubusercontent.com/containers/common/main/pkg/seccomp/seccomp.json

and then try (with `/path/to/the/seccomp.json` pointing to the file you've just downloaded):

```
podman run --security-opt seccomp=/path/to/the/seccomp.json -it --rm docker.io/library/archlinux /bin/bash -c '[ -r /etc/fstab ] && echo "!"'
```

I can't reproduce anymore after switching to seccomp.json you linked to. Looks like this file got forgotten on my end during one of recent upgrades as I can see seccomp.json.pacnew.. Apologies for wasting your time..

A little late, but it looks like ArchLinux isn't updating the seccomp profile?
Just saw this on an ArchLinux system seemingly meeting all the requirements.

Details here:   https://bugs.archlinux.org/task/73680    Not re-opening because it looks like an ArchLinux issue... but wanted to leave some breadcrumbs for future lurkers :-)
